clusters.claim: S1's less-coachable/aptitude assessment must be psychometrically validated in Hungary (no validated instrument exists) with teacher co-design before national rollout, or reform failure gets attributed to those who administer it.
S1's SES-weighted quotas collapse without an administratively workable, gaming-resistant SES proxy, since Hungary has no student-level SES register and self-reported proxies can be gamed.
Cross-maintainer equity mandates must bind church and foundation schools with enforceable sanctions, or advantaged families relocate to non-state channels and sorting is relocated, not reduced.
Uniform equity mandates across maintainers breach institutional autonomy and subsidiarity, treating maintainer plurality as a problem to be solved rather than a feature to preserve.
Reformed formal admission risks substituting opaque informal selection (interviews, reputation) that again favours high-SES families and expands teacher discretion without clear rules.
S1 leaves prior-stage inequity untouched (who applies at all), so its equity gain is bounded and must not be framed as a system-wide fix.

Capping state selective seats without changing entry rules mechanically makes residual selective places even more SES-skewed while non-state maintainers absorb displaced demand, relocating rather than reducing sorting.

A decade-long phase-down creates a parallel-regime 'zombie' period that harms teacher employment security, worsens the teacher shortage via exodus, and locks 10 cohorts into a system deemed inequitable.
Phasing down state selective seats without per-pupil funding support privatises demographic risk onto non-state maintainers, threatening their viability and institutional mission.
S2's peer-composition equity benefit rests on a contested/weak peer-effect mechanism, so its own risk analysis contradicts its equity rationale.
Delaying tracking to age 14 directly targets the primary driver of Hungary's SES gradient, supported by moderate-to-strong cross-national evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006; Pekkarinen et al. 2009).

S3's equity gains are hostage to two unresolved risks it acknowledges but does not resolve: middle-class exit/sorting-migration to non-state and choice channels, and political reversal within an electoral horizon (Polish precedent).

S3 asks teachers to teach larger, heterogeneous classes before the promised Finnish-style bundling (retraining, class-size cuts, diagnostic staff) is delivered, with binding teacher shortage and 'high' unbounded costs making that investment fragile.
Comprehensivisation to age 14 abolishes early merit-based talent identification, violates family liberty/subsidiarity, and mechanically compresses the institutional niches of non-state maintainers.
S4 leaves the sorting mechanism intact and only compensates downstream, so it manages/legitimises inequality rather than solving it and forecloses future structural reform (institutional lock-in).

S4's compensation is durability-fragile — targeted funding is a plausible first-cut target in fiscal consolidation — and no country has demonstrated offsetting SES effects at Hungary's selection age (Portugal did not alter its selection age).
S4 is the only scenario that advances equity through compensatory investment (Portuguese TEIP precedent) without infringing maintainer autonomy, respecting subsidiarity and institutional plurality.
S4 could be acceptable only as a temporary bridge with a binding sunset commitment to structural reform (S3) plus constitutionally-protected funding; as a permanent alternative it is rejected.
